questions.q: Which of the following is **not** part of the definition of an étale morphism of schemes?
questions.explain: Étale = flat + unramified (equivalently: smooth of relative dimension $0$). Properness is a separate condition; étale morphisms are by definition of finite presentation but need not be proper.

questions.q: Consider the map $f\colon \mathbb{A}^1_{\mathbb{C}}\to \mathbb{A}^1_{\mathbb{C}}$, $z\mapsto z^5$. Away from the origin it is étale. Over a point $t\neq 0$, how many geometric points does the fibre have?
questions.explain: The fibre over $t\neq 0$ is $\{z : z^5 = t\}$, i.e. $5$ distinct fifth roots, so the étale degree is $5$. Over $t=0$ the fibre collapses to a single ramified point $z=0$.

questions.q: The map $\operatorname{Spec}\mathbb{F}_p[t,t^{-1}]\to\operatorname{Spec}\mathbb{F}_p[t,t^{-1}]$ sending $t\mapsto t^n$ is étale exactly when:
questions.explain: The derivative $nt^{n-1}$ must be invertible on $\mathbb{G}_m$; over $\mathbb{F}_p$ that holds iff $p\nmid n$. Otherwise the map is inseparable (Frobenius-like) and ramified.

questions.q: Why do we take the prime $\ell$ distinct from the characteristic $p$ of the ground field?
choices: Because $H^i_{\text{ét}}(X,\mathbb{Z}/p)$ sees only $p$-torsion and is badly behaved.
Because working with $\ell = p$ collapses étale cohomology to singular cohomology.
There is no such restriction; any prime works.
Because Frobenius has no action when $\ell = p$.
questions.explain: In characteristic $p$, the étale cohomology $H^i_{\text{ét}}(X,\mathbb{Z}/p^n)$ is pathological (one uses crystalline cohomology instead). For $\ell \neq p$ the theory is well-behaved and gives the right Betti numbers.

questions.q: Let $E$ be a smooth projective curve of genus $g$ over an algebraically closed field. What is $\dim_{\mathbb{Q}_\ell} H^1_{\text{ét}}(E, \mathbb{Q}_\ell)$ for $g = 3$?
questions.explain: For a smooth projective curve of genus $g$, $\dim H^1_{\text{ét}}(E,\mathbb{Q}_\ell) = 2g$. With $g=3$ this is $6$ — matching the classical Betti number $b_1 = 2g$ for a complex curve.

One takes the inverse limit over torsion sheaves $\mathbb{Z}/\ell^n$ to land in $\mathbb{Z}_\ell$-modules, then tensors with $\mathbb{Q}_\ell$ to get a finite-dimensional $\mathbb{Q}_\ell$-vector space.

questions.q: For an endomorphism $f\colon X\to X$ of a smooth projective variety with $\Gamma_f$ transverse to the diagonal, the Grothendieck–Lefschetz trace formula says $\#\mathrm{Fix}(f)$ equals:
questions.explain: The count is an **alternating** sum of traces: odd-degree cohomology contributes with a minus sign, matching the classical topological Lefschetz formula $L(f) = \sum (-1)^i \mathrm{Tr}(f^*\mid H^i)$.

questions.q: Apply the trace formula to $X = \mathbb{P}^2_{\mathbb{F}_q}$ with $q=3$ and $f = F_q$ (geometric Frobenius), so $F^*$ acts as $q^i$ on $H^{2i}$ and odd cohomology vanishes. Compute $\#\mathbb{P}^2(\mathbb{F}_3)$.
Why is transversality between $\Gamma_{F_q}$ and the diagonal automatic in characteristic $p$?
choices: Because $dF_q = 0$ on tangent spaces, so $T_x(\Gamma_{F_q})$ and $T_x(\Delta)$ span $T_x(X\times X)$.
Transversality is never automatic; it has to be imposed.
questions.explain: The derivative of $x\mapsto x^q$ is identically zero, so the tangent space of the graph at a fixed point is just $T_x(\Delta)$'s complement inside $T_x(X\times X)$ — the two subspaces are transverse, and every fixed point contributes $+1$ to the intersection.

questions.q: Let $E/\mathbb{F}_q$ be an elliptic curve and write the Frobenius eigenvalues on $H^1_{\text{ét}}$ as $\alpha, \beta$ with $\alpha\beta = q$. If $q = 5$ and $\alpha + \beta = 3$, compute $\#E(\mathbb{F}_5)$.
questions.explain: $\#E(\mathbb{F}_q) = q + 1 - (\alpha+\beta) = 5 + 1 - 3 = 3$. The trace $\alpha+\beta$ is the standard $a_q$.

questions.q: For a smooth projective variety $X$ over $\mathbb{F}_q$, Deligne's theorem says each eigenvalue $\alpha$ of Frobenius on $H^i_{\text{ét}}$ has $|\alpha| = q^{i/2}$. Give one allowed eigenvalue (as a complex number $a+bi$) on $H^1$ for $q = 4$, assuming the eigenvalue lies on the positive imaginary axis.
questions.explain: On $H^1$ with $q=4$ we need $|\alpha| = \sqrt{4} = 2$. On the positive imaginary axis that is $\alpha = 2i$.

questions.q: Which statement is part of the **Weil conjectures** for a smooth projective $X/\mathbb{F}_q$ of dimension $d$?
choices: The zeta function $Z(X,t)$ is a rational function of $t$.
$Z(X,t)$ has a functional equation relating $t$ and $1/(q^d t)$.
The zeros and poles of $Z(X,t)$ lie at $|t| = q^{-i/2}$ for $0\leq i\leq 2d$ (Riemann hypothesis).
questions.explain: Rationality, functional equation, and the analog of the Riemann hypothesis are the three Weil conjectures (with Betti numbers matching topology a fourth). Étale cohomology with its Frobenius action is what makes them provable.

questions.q: Artin's comparison theorem states that for a smooth projective variety $X$ over $\mathbb{C}$ and any prime $\ell$:
questions.explain: Artin's theorem: étale cohomology of a complex variety agrees with topological singular cohomology after tensoring to $\mathbb{Q}_\ell$. In particular $\dim H^i_{\text{ét}}(X,\mathbb{Q}_\ell)$ is the classical Betti number $b_i$, independent of $\ell$.

questions.q: Using the comparison theorem, compute the $\ell$-adic Betti number $b_2 = \dim_{\mathbb{Q}_\ell} H^2_{\text{ét}}(X,\mathbb{Q}_\ell)$ for $X = \mathbb{P}^1 \times \mathbb{P}^1$ over $\mathbb{C}$.
questions.explain: By Künneth and comparison, $H^2(\mathbb{P}^1\times\mathbb{P}^1) = H^2(\mathbb{P}^1)\otimes H^0(\mathbb{P}^1) \oplus H^0\otimes H^2$, giving $b_2 = 1+1 = 2$. The two classes are the pullbacks of the hyperplane class from each factor.

questions.q: Over $\overline{\mathbb{F}_p}$, why can we NOT use $\ell = p$ in $\ell$-adic cohomology, and what replaces it?
choices: $H^i_{\text{ét}}(X,\mathbb{Q}_p)$ is pathological; crystalline cohomology $H^i_{\text{cris}}(X/W(k))$ is the correct $p$-adic Weil cohomology.
One uses singular cohomology of $X(\mathbb{C})$ instead.
One uses de Rham cohomology of the generic fiber.
questions.explain: Étale cohomology with $\mathbb{Z}/p^n$ coefficients sees Frobenius-kernel pathology in characteristic $p$. Crystalline cohomology (Berthelot–Grothendieck) replaces it, producing a $W(k)$-module with semi-linear Frobenius whose characteristic polynomial matches the $\ell$-adic ones for $\ell\neq p$.
